evals.prompt: I need to test a daily three-way-match control in P2P for the full year. Population is about 18,000 matched invoices. How many samples, how do I pick them, and what do I document?
evals.expected_output: Sample of 25-40 (daily/per-transaction frequency), random selection across the full period including period-end, population completeness validation against the GL/system, and item-level attribute documentation.
assertions: Recommends a sample in the 25-60 range with rationale tied to control frequency and risk
Requires population completeness validation before selection
Requires coverage of the full period including period-end
Specifies item-level attribute documentation, not summary-level

evals.prompt: We found 1 exception in 25 samples of a monthly bank reconciliation review control — one rec wasn't reviewed for 6 weeks. The preparer says it was a one-off during staff leave. Can we call the control effective?
evals.expected_output: No isolated-exception conclusion without root cause analysis and expanded testing. Should expand the sample, evaluate compensating controls, and assess severity considering the account's magnitude; staff-leave root cause suggests a design gap (no backup reviewer).
assertions: Refuses to conclude effective based on the 'one-off' explanation alone
Requires sample expansion with zero further tolerance or concludes deficiency
Identifies the missing backup-reviewer design issue
Evaluates severity using likelihood and magnitude, not just the observed error

evals.prompt: Three deficiencies this year: (1) revenue cut-off control failed twice, revenue account is 60% of materiality exposure, (2) one late timesheet approval, immaterial, (3) ITGC access review missed a quarter for the billing system. Classify them.
evals.expected_output: Deficiency 1: likely significant deficiency or material weakness given magnitude near materiality and repeated failure. Deficiency 2: control deficiency. Deficiency 3: must be aggregated with deficiency 1 since billing ITGC underpins the revenue assertions — combined evaluation may elevate severity.
assertions: Classifies the timesheet item as a simple control deficiency
Evaluates the revenue cut-off failure at significant-deficiency-or-worse severity using maximum exposure
Aggregates the ITGC deficiency with the revenue control failure rather than evaluating in isolation
Applies likelihood x magnitude reasoning and mentions compensating controls

evals.prompt: This is the Jan-May population for the revenue approval control (evals/files/revenue_population.csv), which operates per-transaction (daily frequency). Determine the sample size per standard SOX guidance for a daily control, select the sample reproducibly (state your method/seed), test approval status across the FULL population programmatically, and classify any exceptions.
evals.expected_output: Daily/multiple-times-daily control -> 25 selections (or 40 per some methodologies - state the table used). Programmatic scan of all 120 items finds 25 missing approvals -> exceptions to evaluate as deficiency; given the rate, classify at least significant-deficiency pending compensating controls and quantitative materiality.
assertions: States the standard sample size for a daily control (25, or methodology-stated alternative)
Uses a reproducible selection method (stated seed or systematic interval)
Finds the missing-approval exceptions by scanning the full population with code (25 items)
Classifies the finding severity with reasoning (deficiency vs significant deficiency)
Does not claim the control passed
